model_summary: Selects the six complaint-derived criteria (reduced positive reactivity, guilt about not enjoying, autopilot routines, broad emotional blunting, outward masking, and low motivation) as momentary EMA outcomes and pairs them with eight modifiable, measurable predictors drawn from high-ranked psycho-behavioural and bio-routine levers: behavioral activation, savoring/positive noticing, self-compassion, present-moment awareness, micro-activation energizers, sleep quality (morning diary), social connection, and phone screen-time (passive). Design emphasizes repeated momentary assessment (5 prompts/day) for within-person variance and a 28-day window (≈140 momentary observations + 28 morning diaries) to support gVAR estimation while minimizing redundant constructs.
variable_selection_notes: All six complaint-specific criteria are preserved to keep full clinical resolution. Predictor selection prioritized high HyDe / LLM signals (behavioral activation, savoring, self-compassion, mindfulness, sleep/circadian, micro-practices, social connection) and added an objective passive marker (phone screen time) to index autopilot/distracted behavior. Trade-offs: kept only one broad attention/presence variable (mindfulness/presence) to avoid collinearity with savoring/self-compassion and chose sleep as a daily diary rather than high-frequency passive sleep devices to reduce burden. Passive phone signal included despite potential nonlinearity because it is low-burden and mechanistically linked to autopilot/engagement. Uncertainty/bias: self-report measures may be biased by insight/effortful responding; passive screen time can reflect work demands rather than disengagement (hence flagged as nonlinear).

momentary_ema.notes: Fixed blocks with small random jitter reduce predictability while preserving coverage of daily routines. Use reminders and brief questions (≤ 60s).

daily_diary.notes: Morning diary captures prior-night sleep quality and overall overnight recovery; keep single-item 1–9 Likert for feasibility.

passive_streams.notes: Aggregate passive signals (phone screen minutes) over the interval 'since last prompt' to align with EMAs. Require opt-in and clear privacy notice.

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: With 5 prompts/day × 28 days (≈140 observations) per momentary variable and daily diaries, the dataset should be sufficient for initial gVAR (multivariate lagged models). Prefer within-person standardization (person-mean centering) before gVAR. Consider pruning predictors in follow-up analyses if collinearity inflates VAR parameter uncertainty.
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Use 1–9 anchored Likert scales for all EMA items to maximize within-person variance. Passive screen minutes are continuous and likely provide variance; morning sleep quality is expected to vary daily.
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: Main risks: missed prompts (work/commute), passive stream opt-out, and morning diary noncompliance. Mitigation: brief EMAs, small incentives, adaptive reminder timing, and passive-only fallback if EMA missed (record passively).

safety_notes: No acute self-harm or psychosis signals present in the complaint. If responses during data collection indicate severe distress, suicidal ideation, or acute sleep deprivation with cognitive impairment, trigger safety protocol directing participant to urgent clinical contact. Passive data collection (screen time) requires clear consent and privacy safeguards.

criteria_variables.label: Diminished positive emotional response to pleasant events

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, when something pleasant happened, how much did you feel pleasure or positive emotion? (1 = not at all, 9 = a lot)
measurement.notes: Anchored to positive-event reactivity; if no positive event occurred, instruct to rate 'if one had occurred, how much would you have felt' to avoid missingness.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: person-mean centering; optionally seasonal detrend across weeks; no heavy nonlinear transform

predictor_variables.label: Behavioral activation (intentional pleasurable activity engagement)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Scheduling and completing valued or pleasurable activities increases opportunities for positive reinforcement and can restore positive reactivity and motivation.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Plan a short valued activity (call a friend, 15-min walk, creative hobby) and attempt it; start with graded/brief tasks.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you intentionally do a planned/enjoyable activity? Rate how engaged you were: 1 = not at all, 9 = fully engaged.
measurement.notes: If none planned/done, instruct to rate 1 for engagement; consider an optional event-flag field for binary occurrence.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: person-mean centering; use in raw 1-9 for interpretability

expected_effects.rationale: Doing planned activities should increase momentary motivation/interest and future motivation via momentum.
Completing pleasurable activities provides opportunities for positive affective responses.

feasibility.notes: Single 1–9 item per prompt is low burden and aligns with EMA schedule.

predictor_variables.label: Phone screen-time minutes (passive) — proxy for autopilot/distracted behavior

measurement.notes: Requires explicit consent to collect screen-time; interpret with caution (work-related usage vs autopilot leisure).

expected_effects.rationale: Higher unstructured screen-time may be associated with more autopilot and less presence, but work-related screen time can confound directionality (hence nonlinear).

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Privacy-sensitive; require explicit opt-in and local aggregation (no raw content).

feasibility.notes: Feasible on most smartphones but may be limited by OS permissions and user comfort.

Better sleep reduces daytime automatic behavior and improves presence.

expected_collinearity_risks: P02 (savoring) and P01 (behavioral activation) may correlate when activation includes pleasant activities.
P04 (mindfulness/presence) and P03 (self-compassion) may show moderate correlation in practice (both attention-related regulation skills).
Several EMA outcomes (C01, C04) may covary strongly; consider dimensionality reduction if necessary.
expected_floor_ceiling_risks: C05 (external presentation) may show floor/ceiling effects in some participants (low variability).
P03 (self-compassion) and P02 (savoring) may show skew (many low baseline values) early in the study.
variance_enhancement_suggestions: Use 1–9 Likert anchors and explicit instruction to respond in hypothetical terms if no event occurred to reduce zero-inflation.
Encourage short planned micro-experiments to produce variance (e.g., daily planned pleasant activity).
excluded_or_deprioritized_candidates: Multiple near-duplicate self-regulation constructs (e.g., both lengthy mindfulness scales and brief presence item) — only brief presence included.
Objective continuous sleep device streams (e.g., actigraphy) — excluded to reduce burden and complexity; single-item morning diary included instead.
diagnostics.gvar_feasibility_summary: With 5 prompts/day across momentary variables and 28 days, the within-person time series should provide adequate temporal resolution for gVAR. Person-mean centering and controlling for day-of-week/sleep trends recommended. If multicollinearity arises, drop or combine highly correlated predictors (e.g., P01 vs P05) before final gVAR estimation.

criteria.label: Diminished positive emotional response to pleasant events
criteria.query_text_used: Diminished positive emotional response to pleasant events | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, when something pleasant happened, how much did you feel pleasure or positive emotion? (1 = not at all, 9 = a lot) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Anchored to positive-event reactivity; if no positive event occurred, instruct to rate 'if one had occurred, how much would you have felt' to avoid missingness.

mapping.rationale: Best match: 'anhedonia_nonreactive_to_pleasure' reflects diminished positive reactivity to pleasurable events. The variable asks, after something pleasant happened, how much pleasure/positive emotion was felt (EMA, 1-9). This operationalizes momentary positive affect reactivity; among candidates, this leaf is the most specific clinical counterpart for diminished positive response to enjoyable events.

source_variable.label: Diminished positive emotional response to pleasant events

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, when something pleasant happened, how much did you feel pleasure or positive emotion? (1 = not at all, 9 = a lot)
measurement.notes: Anchored to positive-event reactivity; if no positive event occurred, instruct to rate 'if one had occurred, how much would you have felt' to avoid missingness.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: person-mean centering; optionally seasonal detrend across weeks; no heavy nonlinear transform

criteria.label: Guilt about not enjoying positive experiences
criteria.query_text_used: Guilt about not enjoying positive experiences | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you feel guilty or self-critical about not enjoying something you experienced? (1 = not at all, 9 = extremely) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Targets the specific guilt described in complaint; may correlate with general negative affect but captured as event-specific self-evaluation.

mapping.rationale: C02 maps to event-level guilt about not enjoying experiences. The most specific operational match is guilt_present (idx 29253) under depressive disorders, which represents current guilt feelings during momentary assessments. This aligns with EMA_self_report, since_last_prompt, 1-9 scale, and the targeted guilt event described in the variable. Other guilt leaves capture excessive guilt or guilt_worthlessness, which are broader or more pathology-specific; thus guilt_present is the best single-match leaf for this variable.

source_variable.label: Guilt about not enjoying positive experiences

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you feel guilty or self-critical about not enjoying something you experienced? (1 = not at all, 9 = extremely)
measurement.notes: Targets the specific guilt described in complaint; may correlate with general negative affect but captured as event-specific self-evaluation.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: person-mean centering; consider logit-type transform if heavy floor effects

criteria.label: Behavioral autopilot / low moment-to-moment engagement
criteria.query_text_used: Behavioral autopilot / low moment-to-moment engagement | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, how often did you do things in an automatic or 'on autopilot' way (mindless, not present)? (1 = not at all/present, 9 = entirely on autopilot) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Directly operationalizes autopilot; pairs with passive screen-time to triangulate.

mapping.rationale: Chosen leaf: automatic_behaviors_during_wakefulness (idx 26216). It directly corresponds to the variable label 'Behavioral autopilot / low moment-to-moment engagement' by explicitly encoding automatic or autopilot behaviors during wakefulness. It aligns with the measurement item: EMA self-report assessing 'Since the last prompt, how often did you do things in an automatic or 'on autopilot' way' via smartphone EMA app (5 prompts/day, 1-9 scale). Mechanistically, autopilot reflects reduced present-moment engagement, which can be triangulated with the design's passive phone screen-time metric. Although located in a sleep-wake disorders domain, the construct is a precise operational match for autopilot behavior and is the most specific candidate among the provided leaves.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how often did you do things in an automatic or 'on autopilot' way (mindless, not present)? (1 = not at all/present, 9 = entirely on autopilot)
measurement.notes: Directly operationalizes autopilot; pairs with passive screen-time to triangulate.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: person-mean centering; consider robust scaling

criteria.label: General emotional blunting (low affect intensity)
criteria.query_text_used: General emotional blunting (low affect intensity) | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, how intense were your emotions overall (both positive and negative)? (1 = not intense/very blunted, 9 = very intense) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Captures broad affect intensity; useful to separate blunting from valence-specific changes.

mapping.rationale: Mapping to flat_affect leaf because the variable describes general emotional blunting (low affect intensity). The EMA self-report item measures overall affect intensity (1-9), which directly reflects affective reactivity. flat_affect is a general, clinically recognized construct representing reduced emotional expressivity/intensity across affective domains, making it the best match among candidates. Other leaves are more disorder-specific or describe narrower facets (e.g., blunted_affect_present in schizophrenia, restricted_affective_repertoire). This leaf aligns with the designed repeated momentary assessments (5 prompts/day) to capture within-person variance in affective intensity and overall blunting rather than valence-specific changes.
mapping.leaf_embed_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / movement disorders and other side effects of medication / other (secondary) side effects of medication / organ-system effects / psychiatric or behavioral / emotional blunting / *flat_affect
mapping.leaf_full_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / movement disorders and other side effects of medication / other (secondary) side effects of medication / organ-system effects / psychiatric or behavioral / emotional blunting / flat_affect

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how intense were your emotions overall (both positive and negative)? (1 = not intense/very blunted, 9 = very intense)
measurement.notes: Captures broad affect intensity; useful to separate blunting from valence-specific changes.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: person-mean centering; scale to unit variance

criteria.label: External presentation of being 'fine' despite internal disengagement
criteria.query_text_used: External presentation of being 'fine' despite internal disengagement | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, how much did you present or act as if you were 'fine' while feeling different inside? (1 = not at all, 9 = a lot) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: May show lower within-person variance but preserves complaint-specific outward masking behavior.

mapping.rationale: Maps to outward masking; best leaf 'camouflaging_public_appearance' (idx 28974) with *camouflaging_public_appearance; measurement item asks about presenting as 'fine' despite inner disengagement, directly reflecting public camouflage; mechanism: deliberate display/manipulation of emotional presentation to conceal private disengagement; aligns with the pseudoprofile's outward masking criterion and matches the EMA self-report channel (smartphone app) and 1-9 scale.

source_variable.label: External presentation of being 'fine' despite internal disengagement

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how much did you present or act as if you were 'fine' while feeling different inside? (1 = not at all, 9 = a lot)
measurement.notes: May show lower within-person variance but preserves complaint-specific outward masking behavior.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: person-mean centering if variance sufficient; otherwise treat as slower-moving covariate

criteria.label: Reduced motivation/interest to pursue non-routine pleasurable activities
criteria.query_text_used: Reduced motivation/interest to pursue non-routine pleasurable activities | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, how motivated were you to do something enjoyable beyond routine obligations? (1 = not at all motivated, 9 = highly motivated) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Designed to capture momentary fluctuations in motivational drive relevant to behavioral activation.

mapping.rationale: Best match is idx 5790: intrinsic motivation / internal drive. The variable C06 measures momentary motivation to engage in non-routine enjoyable activities, which maps directly to an intrinsic motivation construct. The EMA self-report (1-9 Likert, five prompts/day) aligns with this leaf's domain, and it is more specific than broader motivation values (e.g., general goal-directed energy) and not PTSD-sexual or trauma-specific (e.g., diminished_interest_in_activities). In addition, this leaf dovetails with the pseudoprofile focus on behavioral activation and low motivation, representing the mechanistic driver of engagement with rewarding activities.

source_variable.label: Reduced motivation/interest to pursue non-routine pleasurable activities

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how motivated were you to do something enjoyable beyond routine obligations? (1 = not at all motivated, 9 = highly motivated)
measurement.notes: Designed to capture momentary fluctuations in motivational drive relevant to behavioral activation.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: person-mean centering; consider AR components in gVAR

predictors.label: Behavioral activation (intentional pleasurable activity engagement)
predictors.query_text_used: Behavioral activation (intentional pleasurable activity engagement) | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Scheduling and completing valued or pleasurable activities increases opportunities for positive reinforcement and can restore positive reactivity and motivation. | Intervention examples: Plan a short valued activity (call a friend, 15-min walk, creative hobby) and attempt it; start with graded/brief tasks. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, did you intentionally do a planned/enjoyable activity? Rate how engaged you were: 1 = not at all, 9 = fully engaged. | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / Cognitive_Behavioral_Therapies / Behavior_Change_and_Activation

mapping.rationale: The variable is 'Behavioral activation (intentional pleasurable activity engagement)' measured via EMA with items about planning and engaging in valued/pleasurable activities. The leaf 'behavioral_activation_scheduling' explicitly targets scheduling and execution of valued activities, matching the measurement item (intentional planning and engagement) and the mechanism (facilitates positive reinforcement and activation). This is the most specific operational construct among candidates for this predictor.

source_variable.label: Behavioral activation (intentional pleasurable activity engagement)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Scheduling and completing valued or pleasurable activities increases opportunities for positive reinforcement and can restore positive reactivity and motivation.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Plan a short valued activity (call a friend, 15-min walk, creative hobby) and attempt it; start with graded/brief tasks.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you intentionally do a planned/enjoyable activity? Rate how engaged you were: 1 = not at all, 9 = fully engaged.
measurement.notes: If none planned/done, instruct to rate 1 for engagement; consider an optional event-flag field for binary occurrence.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: person-mean centering; use in raw 1-9 for interpretability

expected_effects.rationale: Doing planned activities should increase momentary motivation/interest and future motivation via momentum.
Completing pleasurable activities provides opportunities for positive affective responses.

feasibility.notes: Single 1–9 item per prompt is low burden and aligns with EMA schedule.

predictors.label: Savoring / positive-event noticing
predictors.query_text_used: Savoring / positive-event noticing | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Deliberate noticing and savoring of positive moments can amplify positive affective responses and counteract blunting. | Intervention examples: Brief savoring exercise (notice the sensory/details of a pleasant moment for 1-2 minutes). | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, to what extent did you intentionally notice or savor any pleasant moment? (1 = not at all, 9 = very much) | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / Positive_Psychology_and_Wellbeing / Gratitude_and_Savoring

mapping.rationale: Variable label 'Savoring / positive-event noticing' maps to the savoring_positive_moments_practice leaf under PSYCHO / positive_psychology_and_wellbeing / gratitude_and_savoring. It uses EMA_self_report measurement (Since the last prompt) of intentionally noticing or savoring pleasant moments, aligning with a micro-practice mechanism. Deliberate savoring amplifies positive affect and counters blunting, matching the predictor's intended function in the pseudoprofile. This leaf is the most specific, directly representing savoring of positive moments rather than broader related constructs.

source_variable.label: Savoring / positive-event noticing
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Deliberate noticing and savoring of positive moments can amplify positive affective responses and counteract blunting.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Brief savoring exercise (notice the sensory/details of a pleasant moment for 1-2 minutes).

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you intentionally notice or savor any pleasant moment? (1 = not at all, 9 = very much)
measurement.notes: Captures brief micropractices linked to increased positive affect.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: person-mean centering

expected_effects.rationale: Savoring should increase immediate positive affect when it occurs.

predictors.label: Self-compassion / reduced self-criticism
predictors.query_text_used: Self-compassion / reduced self-criticism | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Self-compassion practices target guilt and self-criticism, reducing the secondary distress tied to not enjoying events. | Intervention examples: Brief self-compassion prompt (kind phrase to self; mindful acceptance). | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, to what extent did you respond to yourself with compassion rather than self-criticism? (1 = entirely self-critical, 9 = entirely self-compassionate) | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / Self_and_Identity_Work / Shame_Guilt_and_Self_Forgiveness

mapping.rationale: Direct match: variable label Self-compassion / reduced self-criticism aligns with leaf 'self_compassion_in_shame_moment' under PSYCHO / core_psychological_skills_toolkits / emotion_regulation_and_affective_mastery / difficult_emotion_modules / shame_guilt_anger_jealousy_grief / *self_compassion_in_shame_moment. The EMA item asks about responding with compassion rather than self-criticism since the last prompt, which is an in-the-moment self-compassion index. Mechanism rationale fits: self-compassion practices target guilt and self-criticism, reducing distress; measurement is EMA self-report via smartphone, matching the measurement mode/channel. Pseudoprofile context notes self-compassion as a prioritized predictor, with high relevance to reducing guilt-related distress.

source_variable.label: Self-compassion / reduced self-criticism
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Self-compassion practices target guilt and self-criticism, reducing the secondary distress tied to not enjoying events.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Brief self-compassion prompt (kind phrase to self; mindful acceptance).

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you respond to yourself with compassion rather than self-criticism? (1 = entirely self-critical, 9 = entirely self-compassionate)
measurement.notes: Targets guilt-specific regulation skill; may moderate the impact of low positive reactivity on overall distress.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: person-mean centering

expected_effects.rationale: Increased self-compassion should reduce guilt/self-criticism about low enjoyment.

predictors.label: Momentary present-moment awareness (mindfulness)
predictors.query_text_used: Momentary present-moment awareness (mindfulness) | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Increasing psychological presence reduces autopilot, increases emotional granularity, and supports noticing/savoring. | Intervention examples: Brief 30-second grounding check-in; orient to breath/body. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, how present and aware were you of what you were doing? (1 = not at all, 9 = completely present) | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / Third_Wave_and_Contextual_Therapies / Mindfulness_Based_Interventions

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf is mindfulness_of_thoughts (idx 3781) under present_moment_awareness within the Third Wave/Contextual Therapies mindfulness branch. The variable is Momentary present-moment awareness measured by EMA self-report (5 prompts/day), aiming to capture cognitive, moment-to-moment awareness of ongoing experience. Among candidates, mindfulness_of_thoughts directly targets awareness of current thoughts as a facet of present-moment awareness, whereas other leaves (body scan, breath, emotions) focus on modalities or are practice-focused. Thus this leaf provides the most specific operational construct aligned with the variable.

source_variable.label: Momentary present-moment awareness (mindfulness)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Increasing psychological presence reduces autopilot, increases emotional granularity, and supports noticing/savoring.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Brief 30-second grounding check-in; orient to breath/body.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how present and aware were you of what you were doing? (1 = not at all, 9 = completely present)
measurement.notes: Chosen as the single self-regulation/attention construct to limit redundancy.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: person-mean centering

expected_effects.rationale: Greater presence should reduce autopilot-like behavior in subsequent moments.

feasibility.notes: Short single-item feasible and expected to vary within-day.

predictors.label: Micro-activation (5-minute energizers / small wins)
predictors.query_text_used: Micro-activation (5-minute energizers / small wins) | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Small, frequent activation tasks create momentum, improve energy and motivation, and provide opportunities for positive feedback. | Intervention examples: 5-minute brisk walk, stretch, music boost, or short social check-in. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, did you do a short energizing activity (5 minutes) and how energizing was it? (1 = not at all, 9 = very energizing) | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / Cognitive_Behavioral_Therapies / Behavior_Change_and_Activation / skill_shaping_and_routine

mapping.rationale: Variable maps to Micro-activation as short, frequent activation tasks. Leaf 4095 corresponds to PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / behavior_change_and_activation / skill_shaping_and_routine / building_momentum / small_wins_protocol, which directly matches the concept of 5-minute energizers (micro-wins) designed to build momentum, boost energy/motivation, and provide rapid feedback. The EMA self-report item (since-last-prompt energizing activity) aligns with a momentary assessment of small activations within a routine activation framework.
mapping.leaf_full_path: PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / behavior_change_and_activation / skill_shaping_and_routine / building_momentum / small_wins_protocol

source_variable.label: Micro-activation (5-minute energizers / small wins)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Small, frequent activation tasks create momentum, improve energy and motivation, and provide opportunities for positive feedback.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: 5-minute brisk walk, stretch, music boost, or short social check-in.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you do a short energizing activity (5 minutes) and how energizing was it? (1 = not at all, 9 = very energizing)
measurement.notes: Captures micro-activations that can be rapidly deployed and measured repeatedly.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: person-mean centering; consider binary event flag for sensitivity analyses

expected_effects.rationale: Micro-activations interrupt autopilot and increase subsequent engagement.

feasibility.notes: Frequent binary + intensity reporting possible; single 1–9 item reduces burden.

predictors.label: Sleep quality / last-night restoration (morning diary)
predictors.query_text_used: Sleep quality / last-night restoration (morning diary) | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Nightly sleep quality and circadian regularity influence daytime energy, attention, affect reactivity, and propensity to operate on autopilot. | Intervention examples: Consistent sleep window, light exposure in morning, pre-bed low-stimulation routine. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | How well did you sleep last night / how restorative was your sleep? (1 = very poor, 9 = very restorative) | Prior ontology hint: BIO / Sleep_Circadian_and_Restoration / Sleep_Routines_and_Practices

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf: sleep_quality_rating. The variable is Sleep quality / last-night restoration measured as a single-item morning diary (EMA_self_report, 1-9 Likert). The sleep_quality_rating leaf precisely captures a single-item assessment of sleep quality/restoration within daily logs, aligning with the measurement mode and item semantics.

source_variable.label: Sleep quality / last-night restoration (morning diary)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Nightly sleep quality and circadian regularity influence daytime energy, attention, affect reactivity, and propensity to operate on autopilot.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Consistent sleep window, light exposure in morning, pre-bed low-stimulation routine.

measurement.item_or_signal: How well did you sleep last night / how restorative was your sleep? (1 = very poor, 9 = very restorative)
measurement.notes: Single-item morning diary reduces burden and aligns with circadian hypothesis.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: person-mean centering; consider controlling for weekday/weekend effects

expected_effects.rationale: Better sleep should reduce daytime automatic behavior and increase presence.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: If very poor sleep reported consistently, consider flagging for clinical follow-up.

feasibility.notes: Morning diary improves sleep measurement validity vs recall later in day.

predictors.label: Social connection / brief social contact
predictors.query_text_used: Social connection / brief social contact | Domain: SOCIAL | Mechanism: Brief social contact and perceived connection can increase positive affect and motivation and act as behavioral activation. | Intervention examples: Short supportive message to a friend, 10-minute call, join a community event. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, how connected did you feel to others or did you have meaningful social contact? (1 = not at all, 9 = very connected) | Prior ontology hint: SOCIAL / Social_Support_and_Belonging / Network_Building_And_Maintenance

mapping.rationale: The variable is Social connection / brief social contact measured via EMA with a 1-9 Likert scale (since last prompt). The most specific ontology leaf among candidates that aligns with a brief social contact/micro-connection concept is 1387: SOCIAL / social_support_and_belonging / belonging_isolation_and_meaning / loneliness_reduction_planning / meaningful_activity_with_others_planning, specifically the micro_connection_opportunities_plan subnode. This captures brief social engagement opportunities and the feeling of connection, matching the measurement focus (perceived connection after brief contact) and the mechanism (social contact boosts positive affect and motivation). It is the closest precise fit among the social leaves and avoids broader, less specific mappings.

source_variable.label: Social connection / brief social contact
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Brief social contact and perceived connection can increase positive affect and motivation and act as behavioral activation.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Short supportive message to a friend, 10-minute call, join a community event.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how connected did you feel to others or did you have meaningful social contact? (1 = not at all, 9 = very connected)
measurement.notes: Captures brief social investments that can influence positive affect and motivation.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: person-mean centering

expected_effects.rationale: Social connection increases opportunities for positive affect and reinforcement.

feasibility.notes: Single-item feasible; social contact may be constrained by schedule.

predictors.label: Phone screen-time minutes (passive) — proxy for autopilot/distracted behavior
predictors.query_text_used: Phone screen-time minutes (passive) — proxy for autopilot/distracted behavior | Domain: BIOxSOCIAL | Mechanism: Higher passive phone screen time (unstructured) in short windows may indicate distractibility/autopilot and reduce presence or savoring; relationship may be nonlinear (work vs leisure). | Intervention examples: Phone-use boundary setting, brief phone-free periods, app timers. | Measurement: passive_phone_sensor | Total phone screen time minutes since previous prompt (auto-logged via OS usage API) | Prior ontology hint: BIO / Personalization_Monitoring_and_Data_Feedback / Wearables_and_Home_Monitoring

mapping.rationale: Best-matching predictor leaf: idx 4507 with full path BIO / personalization_monitoring_and_data_feedback / self_assessment_and_logs / context_and_behavioral_context_logs / screen_time_selfreport. The variable is Phone screen-time minutes (passive) as a proxy for autopilot/distracted behavior. This leaf explicitly encodes screen time as a logged self-report of contextual behavior, which is the closest operational construct among candidates to quantify 'screen time' as a predictor of autopilot/distracted engagement. There is no exact passive-phone-sensor leaf in the candidates, so this leaf is chosen for its specificity to screen-time signal and its mechanistic relation to autopilot, while noting the measurement modality differs (self-report vs passive).

source_variable.label: Phone screen-time minutes (passive) — proxy for autopilot/distracted behavior
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Higher passive phone screen time (unstructured) in short windows may indicate distractibility/autopilot and reduce presence or savoring; relationship may be nonlinear (work vs leisure).
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Phone-use boundary setting, brief phone-free periods, app timers.

measurement.channel: phone OS usage API (minutes screen ON/unlocked) aggregated in-app
measurement.item_or_signal: Total phone screen time minutes since previous prompt (auto-logged via OS usage API)
measurement.notes: Requires explicit consent to collect screen-time; interpret with caution (work-related usage vs autopilot leisure).

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: log1p(minutes) or winsorize extreme values; person-mean centering afterwards

expected_effects.rationale: Higher unstructured screen-time may be associated with more autopilot and less presence, but work-related screen time can confound directionality (hence nonlinear).

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Privacy-sensitive; require explicit opt-in and local aggregation (no raw content).

feasibility.notes: Feasible on most smartphones but may be limited by OS permissions and user comfort.
